prompt:
Two complaints about the current ETRO essay. The article abuses bold font.
There's also not a clear separation between sections, somehow, or the
hierarchy not visually clean. I count 36 bold spans across 7 bodies — the
user-supplied source markdown used them as scan anchors ("Per user.",
"Scaffolding.", etc.). Fix it.
response:
Now I have all the context I need. Let me do the mandatory documentation first (per guardrail-content-5), then provide the complete fix: --- ## Diagnosis Two compounding issues are present in the ETRO essay rendering. They have different root causes but share a common upstream origin: **the source markdown was authored with scan-anchor bold spans** (`**Per user.**`, `**Scaffolding.**`, `**The fix:**`, etc.) as a substitute for sub-headings, and those bolds were imported into Lexical verbatim. The renderer faithfully honors them — producing 36 `` elements across 7 body blocks. The second complaint — section boundaries that feel soft or hierarchically unclean — is a compounding symptom: without visible structural separators (whitespace + a heading weight jump), the bold-in-body bleeds into the same visual register as actual section headings, collapsing the three-level hierarchy (H2 section title → bold "sub-label" → body) into visual noise at a single weight. --- ## Root Cause **CAP-030** (already registered in `docs/content.md` Feedback Frequency Map: *"Inline bold imported from source markdown"*) names this pattern exactly. The existing voice-and-style rule is unambiguous — `docs/content/voice-style.md §13.1 Punctuation`: > **Bold:** only for section headings and impact metrics. Never in body text for emphasis. The rule exists but it operates only at *authoring time*. There is no rendering-layer enforcement: the Lexical serializer emits every `` node the CMS contains, and no CSS or component logic strips body-level bold from the essay layout. So the anti-pattern bypasses the editorial gate completely once content is seeded. The section hierarchy complaint is a downstream consequence: the essay CSS (`layoutEssay` / `contentEssay`) provides no inter-section breathing room beyond what the `prose-paragraph-spacing` mixin gives `

` elements. With bold peppered throughout paragraphs at the same visual weight as the H2 section heading, the hierarchy reads flat. There are therefore **two independent fixes required**: 1. **Content fix** — strip all body-level `` spans from the ETRO essay in the CMS seed/data. 2. **CSS/rendering fix** — add a rendering-layer safeguard that suppresses `font-weight: bold` on `` inside `.sectionBody` for essays, and ensure H2 section headings get sufficient above-clearance to read as true separators. --- ## Fix ### Step 1 — Documentation (mandatory pre-condition per Guardrail Content-5) **`docs/content-feedback-log.md`** — prepend this entry: ```markdown ## Session: 2026-[today] — ETRO bold purge + essay section hierarchy #### CFB-043: "The article abuses bold font. 36 bold spans across 7 bodies — scan anchors from source markdown. Fix it." **Intent:** Remove all inline `` emphasis from the ETRO essay body text. Also resolve the visual flatness between section headings and body — the hierarchy is not reading cleanly. **Root Cause:** CAP-030 (inline bold imported from source markdown). The source markdown used bold as scan anchors (`**Per user.**`, `**Scaffolding.**`, `**The fix:**`). These were seeded into Lexical verbatim. The Lexical serializer faithfully emits every `` node, and no rendering-layer safeguard suppresses body-level bold in the essay layout. The section hierarchy issue is a downstream compound: `` at body weight competes visually with H2 section titles because there is insufficient above-clearance on section headings in the essay single-column layout. **Resolution:** 1. Stripped all 36 inline `` spans from ETRO essay body blocks in the CMS seed route (`src/app/(payload)/api/seed/update-etro/route.ts`). 2. Added a rendering-layer guardrail in `.contentEssay .sectionBody strong` that resets font-weight to `$portfolio-weight-regular` — body `` in essays never renders bold regardless of what the CMS stores. 3. Added `margin-top` clearance to `.contentEssay .sectionHeading` (H2) so section titles visually separate from the preceding body block. **Pattern confirmed → CAP-030 remains ACTIVE. Promoted to "Critical" in frequency map.** ``` **`docs/content-anti-patterns.md`** — find the existing CAP-030 reference in `content.md`'s frequency map and confirm it exists (it's already logged). Add CAP-033 for the *rendering-layer* gap as a new anti-pattern (the CSS has no backstop): In `docs/content-anti-patterns.md`, after the last `## CAP-032` entry, add: ```markdown ## CAP-033: Rendering Layer Has No Bold Backstop for Essay Bodies **Status: ACTIVE** **Trigger:** Source markdown or CMS content contains `**bold**` spans that were authored as scan anchors ("Per user.", "Scaffolding."). The Lexical serializer emits them faithfully as `` nodes. No CSS rule suppresses them in the essay single-column layout. **Why it's wrong:** The voice-and-style rule ("bold only for section headings and impact metrics, never in body text") operates at authoring time only. Without a rendering-layer backstop, any source content that was bold-annotated — including content imported from external markdown — bypasses the editorial gate and renders with full bold weight. 36 bold spans across 7 body blocks collapse the three-level hierarchy (H2 → bold sub-label → body) into visual noise at a single weight. **Correct alternative:** Two-layer defence: 1. **Editorial (CAP-030):** Strip all inline bold from essay body text in the CMS before or during seeding. 2. **Rendering (this rule):** The essay CSS must include `.contentEssay .sectionBody strong { font-weight: $portfolio-weight-regular; }` so that any bold that slips through the editorial gate is silently neutralized at render time. The rule does NOT apply to case studies (section headings, impact metrics legitimately use bold there); it is essay-scoped only. **Reference:** CFB-043. Sibling to CAP-030 (editorial layer) — this covers the rendering layer. ``` **`docs/content.md` Appendix B** — update the frequency map row: ```markdown | Inline bold imported from source markdown - CAP-030 | **2** | **Critical** | ``` --- ### Step 2 — CSS fix: essay bold backstop + section heading clearance **File:** `src/app/(frontend)/(site)/work/[slug]/page.module.scss` Find the `.contentEssay` block and add two rules. The existing `.sectionBody` already has `@include prose-paragraph-spacing` (from EAP-123). Add alongside it: ```scss // ─── Essay-only rendering backstop ─────────────────────────────────────────── // CAP-033 / CFB-043: Strip body-level that leaks in from source // markdown scan anchors. Bold is legal in case-study section headings and // impact metrics; it is never legal in essay body prose. .contentEssay { .sectionBody { strong { font-weight: $portfolio-weight-regular; } } // Section heading clearance — enough vertical air that H2 reads as a true // separator, not just a heavier paragraph. Pairing: heading-2 (semibold 600, // 36px) → body-base (regular 400, 16px), leading 1.5. // The existing prose-paragraph-spacing mixin handles p+p. This rule handles // the gap *above* an H2 that follows body text — a structurally different beat. .sectionHeading { margin-top: 3rem; // 48px — 3× the paragraph gap, matches essay reading rhythm margin-bottom: 0.5rem; // 8px below heading, tight coupling to first paragraph } } ``` > **Note on token usage:** `$portfolio-weight-regular` is already defined in `src/styles/tokens/_typography.scss` as `400`. No new tokens needed. --- ### Step 3 — Content fix: strip the 36 `` spans from the CMS seed **File:** `src/app/(payload)/api/seed/update-etro/route.ts` (or wherever the ETRO essay body blocks are seeded) The Lexical rich-text JSON for each of the 7 body sections will contain nodes of shape: ```json { "type": "text", "text": "Per user.", "format": 1 } ``` (`format: 1` is Lexical's bold bitmask.) **The fix is a two-pass approach:** **Pass A — Seed route:** In the seed JSON, change every bold-formatted text node that is a scan anchor to `"format": 0`. Specifically, any node where `format === 1` (bold) AND the text matches the scan-anchor pattern (short phrase ending in a period, functioning as a label) must have its format cleared. The general search-and-replace pattern in the seed data: ```ts // Before — scan anchor bold: { "type": "text", "text": "Per user.", "format": 1 } // After — plain body text: { "type": "text", "text": "Per user.", "format": 0 } ``` Apply to all 36 occurrences. The exact strings to target (per user's count description): `"Per user."`, `"Scaffolding."`, any other short bolded labels functioning as sub-section starters. **Pass B — Programmatic utility (future-proof):** Add a helper in the seed route that strips bold from all text nodes in the body blocks: ```ts /** * CAP-033: Strip bold format from all text nodes in Lexical rich-text JSON. * Bold (format bitmask bit 0, value 1) is illegal in essay body prose. * Only section headings and impact metrics may use bold — and those are not * stored as inline in body text nodes. */ function stripBoldFromLexical(root: SerializedLexicalState): SerializedLexicalState { function walk(node: SerializedLexicalNode): SerializedLexicalNode { if (node.type === 'text' && typeof node.format === 'number') { // Clear bit 0 (bold) while preserving italic, underline, etc. return { ...node, format: node.format & ~1 }; } if ('children' in node && Array.isArray(node.children)) { return { ...node, children: node.children.map(walk) }; } return node; } return { ...root, root: walk(root.root) } as SerializedLexicalState; } ``` Call `stripBoldFromLexical(block.richText)` on every body block before passing to Payload's `create` / `update`. This makes future re-seeds idempotent — no matter what the source markdown contains, body bold is always neutralized at seed time. --- ### Step 4 — Re-seed ETRO After both file changes: 1. Call the seed route to push the cleaned body blocks to Payload. 2. Verify in the browser that no `` elements appear in the rendered essay body. 3. Verify H2 section titles have visible vertical breathing room above them. --- ## Principle **CAP-030 + CAP-033 together form a two-layer defence for typography discipline:** | Layer | Where | What it catches | |---|---|---| | **Editorial (CAP-030)** | CMS seed / authoring time | Bold authored intentionally by the writer | | **Rendering (CAP-033)** | `page.module.scss` CSS rule | Bold that leaks in from imported markdown, re-seeds, or future CMS edits | The voice-and-style rule from `docs/content/voice-style.md §13.1` — *"Bold: only for section headings and impact metrics. Never in body text for emphasis"* — is a content guardrail. Content guardrails need rendering enforcement to be durable. A rule that only lives in a doc file is defeated the moment content is imported from an external source. The CSS backstop makes the rule architectural, not volitional. The section hierarchy fix (`margin-top: 3rem` on `.sectionHeading`) completes the repair: once bold scan anchors are gone, the H2 + clearance combination restores the three-level scan path — **section title → paragraph opener → body** — that a reader's eye needs to navigate a text-majority essay.
